Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Gastroenterology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 25 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Gastroenterology.

25articles that week
5journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Gastroenterology

AGA Clinical Practice Update on Risk Stratification and Emerging Surveillance Strategies for Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Expert Review

Rich NE, Villanueva A, Marrero JA et al. · 2026 Apr 15
Study Type: Expert review and clinical practice update
Key Question: What are the best practices for hepatocellular carcinoma risk stratification and surveillance strategies in at-risk populations?
Key Findings:
  • Current surveillance (6-monthly ultrasound plus α-fetoprotein) remains standard but has suboptimal sensitivity, with <25% of cirrhotic patients receiving appropriate surveillance
  • Novel biomarkers like GALAD are commercially available but lack sufficient validation for routine use and should not replace guideline-recommended tests
  • For chronic hepatitis B without cirrhosis, PAGE-B and REAL-B scores can effectively stratify HCC risk, but surveillance is not recommended for non-cirrhotic patients with other liver disease aetiologies
Clinical Relevance: Provides updated guidance for UK gastroenterologists on HCC surveillance implementation, particularly relevant given rising HCC burden in non-viral liver disease (MASLD, alcohol-related) within NHS patient populations.
Limitations: Expert opinion-based without systematic literature review, so recommendations lack formal evidence quality ratings.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Pre-Crohn's Disease Stool from Discordant Siblings Promotes the Development of Colitis in Germ-Free Mice

Dang CP, Chen-Liaw A, Xue M et al. · 2026 Apr 17
Study Type: Experimental animal study using germ-free mice
Key Question: Does the gut microbiome from individuals who later develop Crohn's disease promote colitis development compared to healthy siblings?
Key Findings:
  • Germ-free mice colonised with pre-Crohn's stool showed increased weight loss, elevated fecal lipocalin-2, and worse histological damage compared to mice receiving healthy sibling stool
  • 40 metabolites differed between pre-CD and healthy control colonised mice, with sphingolipids showing positive correlation between humans and mice and association with increased colitis severity
  • Pre-disease microbiome changes were detectable years before clinical Crohn's diagnosis
Clinical Relevance: This provides experimental evidence that microbiome alterations precede Crohn's disease development, potentially informing early intervention strategies and microbiome-targeted therapies in high-risk individuals within UK IBD services.
Limitations: Findings from T-cell transfer colitis in germ-free mice may not fully translate to human Crohn's disease pathogenesis.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

CLONAL LYMPHOCYTE EXPANSIONS AND JAK-STAT PATHWAY MUTATIONS DEFINE A PATHOGENIC CONTINUUM DRIVING RESISTANCE TO GLUTEN-FREE DIET IN CELIAC DISEASE

Malamut G, Guégan N, Carbone F et al. · 2026 Apr 17
Study Type: Laboratory-based translational study with single-cell genomic analysis
Key Question: What are the molecular mechanisms driving refractory celiac disease (RCD) subtypes and could JAK inhibitors be therapeutic targets?
Key Findings:
  • RCD2 malignant lymphocytes showed substantial intrapatient heterogeneity, and JAK inhibitors selected for drug-resistant tumour cells in vitro, suggesting monotherapy limitations
  • RCD1 patients harboured clonal T-cell expansions with JAK-STAT pathway-enhancing mutations, indicating shared pathogenic mechanisms with RCD2
  • One patient initially diagnosed as RCD1 developed both RCD2 and CD4+ lymphoproliferation, demonstrating disease evolution potential
Clinical Relevance: This challenges the traditional RCD1/RCD2 classification by revealing a pathogenic continuum, potentially informing treatment strategies for UK patients with refractory celiac disease who don't respond to gluten-free diet.
Limitations: In vitro JAK inhibitor findings may not translate directly to clinical efficacy or safety in patients.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Protein misfolding enteropathy predicts and prognosticates neurodegenerative disease years before symptom onset

Langerová T, MacVicar E, Press R et al. · 2026 Apr 19
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Can protein misfolding detected in routine gastrointestinal biopsies predict neurodegenerative disease development years before symptom onset?
Key Findings:
  • Protein misfolding enteropathy (misfolded TDP-43, tau, α-synuclein) was present in 60% of patients with unexplained GI symptoms, appearing 6.9 years before neurological symptoms
  • High sensitivity (>80%) for predicting non-Alzheimer's dementia or α-synucleinopathies, though specificity was low
  • Presence of ≥2 proteinopathy markers showed dose-dependent association with reduced survival
Clinical Relevance: This could revolutionise early detection of neurodegenerative diseases using routine GI biopsies already performed in NHS gastroenterology services, potentially enabling earlier intervention when treatments may be more effective.
Limitations: Low specificity limits clinical utility due to high false-positive rates.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Achalasia and risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma in a multinational study

Leijonmarck W, Santoni G, Holmberg D et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Population-based case-control study
Key Question: Does achalasia increase the risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma after adjusting for established risk factors?
Key Findings:
  • Achalasia strongly increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma (OR 9.46, 95% CI 7.07-12.65), with even higher risk following myotomy/dilatation (OR 16.33, 95% CI 11.26-23.67)
  • Risk of adenocarcinoma was moderate (OR 3.05, 95% CI 2.14-4.35) but became non-significant after GERD adjustment (OR 1.44, 95% CI 0.99-2.09)
  • Analysis included 35,604 oesophageal cancer cases from five Nordic countries over 36 years
Clinical Relevance: This large-scale evidence supports enhanced squamous cell carcinoma surveillance in UK achalasia patients, particularly post-intervention, while suggesting adenocarcinoma risk is primarily mediated through concurrent GERD.
Limitations: Registry-based design may underestimate achalasia prevalence and cannot capture detailed smoking/alcohol exposure history.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Design of Treatment Trials for Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction

Sayuk GS, Brenner DM, Carbone F et al. · 2026 May
Study Type: Commentary/guidance article
Key Question: What are the best practices for designing and conducting treatment trials in disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBI)?
Key Findings:
  • Most DGBI trial evidence comes from adult IBS studies, with limited progress in less common DGBI conditions
  • Randomized placebo-controlled trials remain the gold standard, though pragmatic trials now demonstrate real-world efficacy
  • Trial design has expanded beyond pharmacotherapy to include dietary interventions and brain-gut behavioural therapies with specific methodological considerations
Clinical Relevance: This guidance addresses critical trial design challenges in DGBI research that UK gastroenterologists encounter when interpreting evidence or participating in studies of functional gut disorders.
Limitations: As a methodology paper, it provides guidance rather than new clinical evidence.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Functional Esophageal Disorders

Gyawali CP, Roman S, Zerbib F et al. · 2026 May
Study Type: Commentary/review article
Key Question: What are the underlying mechanisms and therapeutic approaches for functional esophageal disorders presenting with typical symptoms but no structural abnormalities?
Key Findings:
  • Functional esophageal disorders result from visceral hypersensitivity due to peripheral/central neural dysfunction, potentially triggered by low-grade inflammation
  • Hypervigilance impairs sensory signal interpretation and is modulated by cognitive, affective, and psychosocial factors
  • Treatments targeting motor abnormalities or acid suppression show limited benefit, while neuromodulation approaches appear more promising but lack robust outcome data
Clinical Relevance: This challenges conventional acid-suppression approaches for functional dyspepsia-like esophageal symptoms, suggesting UK gastroenterologists should consider neuromodulatory treatments and psychological interventions for patients with normal endoscopy and pH studies.
Limitations: The review acknowledges sparse outcome data for the promising neuromodulatory therapeutic approaches discussed.
PubMed →DOI →
Gastroenterology

Biopsychosocial Aspects of Adult and Pediatric Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction

Elsenbruch S, Ballou S, Keefer L et al. · 2026 May
Study Type: Narrative review
Key Question: What evidence-based psychosocial approaches should clinicians use when assessing and managing adult and pediatric patients with disorders of gut-brain interaction?
Key Findings:
  • Establishes a biopsychosocial framework demonstrating bidirectional influences between biological and psychosocial factors on gut physiology and brain mechanisms
  • Provides structured assessment strategies including targeted interview questions and response interpretation guidance
  • Outlines empirically supported psychosocial treatments applicable in both integrated gastroenterology services and standalone psychological care settings
Clinical Relevance: Offers practical guidance for UK gastroenterologists to incorporate evidence-based psychosocial assessment and management into DGBI care, potentially improving patient outcomes in this complex patient group where traditional biomedical approaches often prove insufficient.
Limitations: As a narrative review, it does not provide systematic evaluation of evidence quality or quantitative synthesis of treatment effectiveness data.
PubMed →DOI →
